prompt: Match each role to its responsibility (drag the role onto the responsibility).

items.text: Defines user stories and acceptance criteria.
Collaborates to deliver potentially shippable increments.
Protects the team from outside interruptions.

explanation: The product owner defines the scope/value (user stories & acceptance criteria); the development team builds the increments; the scrum master is a servant leader who removes impediments and protects the team.

prompt: Match each item to the methodology that uses it.

items.text: User Stories
Blended Reporting
EVM (Earned Value Management)
Lessons Learned Register

explanation: User stories are agile; 'blended' reporting mixes approaches (hybrid); EVM is mostly used on traditional projects; every project keeps a lessons learned register (all).

prompt: Match each item to the methodology that uses it.

items.text: Daily Standups
Rolling Wave Planning
WBS (Work Breakdown Structure)
Risk Planning and Identification

explanation: Daily standups are agile; rolling wave planning (plan as you learn) fits hybrid; the WBS is traditional; risk planning/identification happens on all projects.

prompt: Match each action to the methodology it describes.

items.text: Follows a sequential phase approach from initiation to closure.
Delivers work in iterative cycles.
Uses a project management plan and a backlog.

explanation: Sequential phases = traditional; iterative delivery = agile; using both a PM plan (traditional) and a backlog (agile) = hybrid.

prompt: Match each impediment to the action that addresses it.

items.text: Communication Breakdown
Scope Creep
items.answer: Implement a change control process to formally review and approve changes.

items.text: Resource Shortage
items.answer: Adjust allocations or seek additional support.

items.text: Risk Realization
Lack of Clear Requirements
items.answer: Conduct a meeting to gather and record precise needs.

options: Implement a change control process to formally review and approve changes.
Adjust allocations or seek additional support.
Conduct a meeting to gather and record precise needs.
explanation: A communication management plan fixes communication breakdowns; a change control process stops scope creep; reallocating/adding support handles resource shortages; when a risk materializes you execute its response plan; unclear requirements are resolved by meeting to gather precise needs.

prompt: Match each person's description to the MBTI personality indicator (drag the indicator onto the description).

items.text: Sarah thrives in team meetings, actively participates, and gains energy from interacting with colleagues.
David approaches decisions logically and objectively, analyzing data and valuing fairness and consistency.
Rachel is organized, likes a clear plan, structure, established procedures, and deadlines.
Lisa is detail-oriented and focuses on concrete facts and practical, present-situation information.

explanation: Energy from interacting = Extraversion; logical/fairness decisions = Thinking; structure/plans/deadlines = Judging; concrete facts/details = Sensing.

prompt: Match each problem to the tool that helps solve it.

items.text: Missed Deadlines
Team members are assigned more work than they can handle.
Unclear Task Dependencies
Frequent Scope Adjustments
Inaccurate Time Estimates

explanation: A Gantt chart shows start/end dates to avoid missed deadlines; resource leveling prevents overloading people; a network diagram clarifies task dependencies; a change control process curbs frequent scope changes; PERT improves time estimates.

prompt: Match each risk attribute to the action that describes it.

items.text: Measures the time until the risk impacts the project.
Assesses the speed at which a risk requires a response.
Identifies how risks are interconnected with other project risks.
Evaluates the closeness of the risk event to the present time.

explanation: Dormancy = time before a risk that has occurred starts to impact; Urgency = how fast a response is needed; Connectivity = how risks are interconnected; Proximity = how close the risk is to occurring.

prompt: Match each item to the methodology that uses it.

items.text: Gantt Chart
Phase Gate Reviews
Sprint Planning
Stakeholder Analysis

explanation: Gantt charts are traditional; phase gate reviews fit hybrid; sprint planning is agile; stakeholder analysis is done on all projects.

prompt: Match each item to the methodology that uses it.

items.text: Mixed Deliverables
Resource Management Plan
Incremental Delivery
Resource Management

explanation: Mixed deliverables = hybrid; a formal management plan = traditional; incremental delivery = agile; managing resources happens on all projects.

prompt: Match each problem to the tool/technique that helps solve it.

items.text: Uncoordinated Task Handovers
Meeting Going Off Topic
Activities Dependency Issues
Unplanned Work
Unclear Task Prioritization

explanation: A Kanban board makes handovers/work-in-progress visible; time-boxing keeps meetings on topic; PDM shows activity dependencies; rolling wave planning handles unplanned/emerging work; MoSCoW prioritizes work (must/should/could/won't).

prompt: Match each task to the collaboration tool that best supports it.

items.text: Coordinate collaborative editing of deliverables to ensure version consistency.
Display project milestones and timelines for team members to track progress.
Organize a project status update meeting with remote team members and stakeholders.
Facilitate real-time discussion for quick decision-making during a critical phase.
Monitor team members' work hours and project time allocation to manage resources.

explanation: Version control keeps deliverable versions consistent; bulletin boards display milestones; video conferencing runs status meetings; chat rooms enable real-time discussion; time tracking software manages work hours.

prompt: Match each description to the role (note: more roles than descriptions — some are distractors).

items.text: Defines acceptance criteria for project deliverables in the product backlog.
Develops detailed project plans, including schedules, budgets, and resource allocation.
Maps out existing business processes to identify areas for improvement.

explanation: 'Acceptance criteria in the product backlog' signals agile = product owner; detailed plans/schedules/budgets is the (traditional) project manager; mapping business processes for improvement is the business analyst. Project Sponsor and Functional Manager are distractors.

prompt: Match each risk attribute to the action that describes it.

items.text: Determines the potential delay before the risk materializes.
Prioritizes risks that need immediate action.
Maps the risk dependencies to understand the potential ripple effects.
Gauges how imminent the risk event is to project execution.

explanation: Delay before it materializes = Dormancy; immediate-action priority = Urgency; ripple effects/dependencies = Connectivity; how imminent/close = Proximity.

prompt: Match each risk attribute to the action that describes it.

items.text: Identifies the latency period of the risk.
Evaluates the required response time for the risk.
Analyzes the links between this risk and others within the project.
Assesses how soon the risk is expected to affect the project.

explanation: Latency period = Dormancy; required response time = Urgency; links between risks = Connectivity; how soon it will affect the project = Proximity.

prompt: Match each responsibility to the role.

items.text: Removes external forces to keep the team productive.
Determines the order of the work.
Determines how to do the actual work needed to build the product.

explanation: The scrum master removes impediments; the product owner prioritizes/orders the work (backlog); the development team decides how to do the work.

prompt: Match each situation to the interpersonal skill it demonstrates.

items.text: Convincing stakeholders to adopt a proposed project plan.
Reaching a compromise with a vendor on contract terms.
A team member fully understands another's concerns in a meeting.
Mediating a disagreement between two team members to find a solution.
A leader empathizes with a stressed team member to provide support.

explanation: Convincing = influencing; compromising on terms = negotiation; fully understanding someone = active listening; mediating a disagreement = conflict resolution; empathizing = emotional intelligence.

prompt: Match each action to the methodology it describes.

items.text: Team does daily stand-ups together with a WBS.
Adapts to changing requirements even late in development.
Conducts detailed WBS and Gantt chart planning.

explanation: Standups (agile) + WBS (traditional) = hybrid; embracing late changes = agile; detailed WBS/Gantt planning = traditional.

prompt: Sort each attribute statement into Predictive or Agile.

items.text: Requirements are fixed and defined at the beginning of the project.
Requirements are evolving and refined throughout the project.
Changes are managed through formal change control processes.
Changes are embraced and incorporated continuously.
Risk and cost are assessed and planned for upfront.
Risk and cost are managed iteratively.
Key stakeholders have limited involvement after initial planning.
Key stakeholders are actively involved throughout the project.
Project scope is clearly defined and controlled.
Project scope is flexible and adaptive.

explanation: Predictive fixes requirements/scope upfront, manages changes via formal change control, plans risk/cost upfront, and engages stakeholders mainly early. Agile keeps requirements/scope evolving, welcomes continuous change, manages risk/cost iteratively, and involves stakeholders throughout.

prompt: Match each task to the collaboration tool that best supports it.

items.text: Track and merge code changes from multiple developers working on the same project.
Edit project documents in real-time with team members.
Collect and display team feedback and suggestions on project processes.
Resolve immediate project issues or roadblocks through instant messaging.
Conduct project kickoff meetings with geographically dispersed team members and stakeholders.

explanation: Version control merges multiple developers' code; collaboration tools enable real-time co-editing; bulletin boards collect/display feedback; chat rooms handle instant messaging; video conferencing runs dispersed kickoff meetings.

prompt: Match each schedule problem to the tool that helps with it.

items.text: Overlapping Activities
How Far Off Schedule
Fragmented Work
Unrealistic Deadlines
Inconsistent Reporting

explanation: CPM shows overlapping activities/sequence; EVM (SPI/SV) shows how far off schedule you are; the WBS breaks down fragmented work; the schedule baseline sets realistic start/finish dates; status meetings fix inconsistent reporting.

prompt: Match each Tuckman team-development stage to its description.

items.text: Initial stage where team members are polite and oriented towards the task.
Characterized by power struggles and competition among team members.
Team members develop stronger relationships and a sense of cohesion.
Team members work collaboratively and effectively towards common goals.
Final stage where team members reflect on achievements and transition out.

explanation: Tuckman's ladder: Forming (polite/orienting) → Storming (power struggles) → Norming (cohesion) → Performing (effective collaboration) → Adjourning (wrap up and disband).

prompt: Match each item to the methodology it describes.

items.text: The PM uses a plan for the near term while keeping future tasks high-level in a product backlog.
When a significant change request is submitted, the PM convenes a meeting to evaluate and approve/reject it.
At the end of each sprint, the team delivers a potentially shippable increment for review and feedback.

explanation: Using a PM plan plus a product backlog = hybrid; approving/rejecting changes via formal change control = traditional; shippable increment each sprint = agile.

prompt: Match each item to the methodology it describes.

items.text: Each morning the team gathers for a brief meeting to share what they did yesterday.
The PM gathers all stakeholders to outline project objectives, deliverables, schedule, and roles.
The PM involves stakeholders continuously using Agile practices while maintaining a structured scope.

explanation: A daily standup = agile; a big upfront plan outlining everything = traditional; agile feedback practices with a structured/fixed scope = hybrid.

prompt: Match each Tuckman team-development stage to its description.

items.text: Team members are learning about the project and their roles.
Frustrations and disagreements are common as the team navigates challenges.
The team builds trust and establishes working agreements.
High productivity and synergy characterize this stage.
Team members celebrate accomplishments and prepare for future endeavors.

explanation: Learning roles = Forming; frustrations/disagreements = Storming; trust/working agreements = Norming; high productivity/synergy = Performing; celebrating and wrapping up = Adjourning.

prompt: Match each description to the role (more roles than descriptions — some are distractors).

items.text: Assists the team in identifying and resolving risks.
Proactively identifies, assesses, and mitigates risks throughout the project lifecycle.
Makes high-level decisions and approves major project changes.
Approves resources to a project manager.

explanation: Assisting the team = scrum master; proactively managing risk throughout = project manager; high-level decisions/approving major changes = sponsor; approving/controlling resources = functional manager. Business Analyst is a distractor.

prompt: Match each action to the methodology it describes.

items.text: Adapts and customizes practices based on project needs.
Uses a detailed project plan and timeline.
Uses sprints to manage work.

explanation: Adapting/customizing a blend of practices = hybrid; a detailed plan/timeline = traditional; sprints = agile.

prompt: Match each description to the tool/technique.

items.text: Monitoring progress and predicting completion.
Adjusting activities within resource constraints.
Identifying and managing task relationships.

explanation: A burn down chart tracks progress and predicts completion; smoothing adjusts activities within resource limits (without extending the critical path); dependency mapping manages task relationships.

prompt: Match each agile document/artifact to its description.

items.text: Captures target audience demographics.
Understanding customer perception of value.
User feedback reports reviewed to improve the process.

explanation: User personas capture the target audience/demographics; user stories express customer value; the sprint retrospective reviews feedback to improve.

prompt: Match each Tuckman team-development stage to its description.

items.text: Focus is on orientation and setting initial expectations.
Conflicts and confrontations arise as team dynamics are tested.
The team finds consensus and begins to function more smoothly.
The team reaches peak performance with effective collaboration.
The team completes the project and disbands, reflecting on their work.

explanation: Orientation/expectations = Forming; conflicts = Storming; consensus/smoother function = Norming; peak performance = Performing; completion/disbanding = Adjourning.

prompt: Match each risk response to the action that describes it.

items.text: Adjust plans to remove the risk or its effects.
Assign the risk to another party, like through insurance.
Acknowledge the risk and choose to deal with it if it happens.
Take steps to lessen the risk's likelihood or impact.
Hand the risk over to someone with more authority.

explanation: Removing the risk entirely = Avoid; assigning to a third party (insurance) = Transfer; acknowledging but not acting unless it happens = Accept; reducing probability/impact = Mitigate; pushing to higher authority = Escalate. (Avoid removes the risk; Mitigate only reduces it.)

prompt: Match each technique to its description.

items.text: Detailed near-term planning while keeping long-term planning flexible.
Prioritizing tasks based on must-have, should-have, could-have, and won't-have criteria.
Analyzing resource allocation over time.

explanation: Detail now, defer long-term detail = rolling wave planning; must/should/could/won't = MoSCoW; a histogram (bar chart) shows resource allocation over time.

prompt: Match each item to the methodology it describes.

items.text: At the start of a new sprint, the team meets to discuss tasks for the upcoming iteration.
The PM finalizes the project scope and obtains formal approval from stakeholders.
The team delivers work in short iterations while planning subsequent phases in detail.

explanation: Sprint planning = agile; finalizing/approving scope upfront = traditional; short iterations plus detailed phase planning = hybrid.

prompt: Match each Tuckman team-development stage to its description.

items.text: The team meets and starts to understand the project and each other.
Internal conflicts occur as team members push boundaries.
Norms are established, and the team begins to work more harmoniously.
The team works efficiently towards achieving project objectives.
The project ends, and the team parts ways or transitions to new tasks.

explanation: Meeting/understanding = Forming; internal conflicts = Storming; norms/harmony = Norming; efficient work = Performing; ending/parting = Adjourning.

prompt: Match each impediment to its description.

items.text: Team members feel disengaged or unmotivated.
Tasks are not completed on time.
Team lacks necessary skills for certain tasks.
Overlapping roles leading to confusion.
Team members have unresolved disagreements.

explanation: Disengaged = low engagement; late tasks = missed deadlines; lacking skills = insufficient expertise; overlapping/confused roles = unclear responsibilities; unresolved disagreements = ongoing disputes.

prompt: Match each item to the methodology that uses it.

items.text: Milestone Chart
Continuous Integration
Flexible and Fixed Scope Management

explanation: Milestone charts (planned milestones upfront) = traditional; continuous integration = agile; managing both flexible and fixed scope = hybrid.

prompt: Match each risk category to its description.

items.text: Pertains to system integration, testing, and validation processes.
Focuses on cost-benefit analysis, ROI, and market entry strategies.
Involves project timelines, deliverable tracking, and resource management.
Considers partnerships, stakeholder expectations, and market regulations.

explanation: Integration/testing/validation = Technical; cost/ROI/market = Commercial; timelines/tracking/resources = Management; partnerships/regulations/outside factors = External.

prompt: Match each action to the methodology it describes.

items.text: Implements regular retrospectives for process improvement.
Integrates Agile's iterative development with traditional risk management.
Uses the critical path method (CPM) for schedule management.

explanation: Retrospectives = agile; combining agile iteration with traditional practices = hybrid; CPM = traditional.

prompt: Match each term to its description.

items.text: Captures and distributes project knowledge and best practices.
Standardizes project management across the organization.
Defines roles, responsibilities, and processes for project oversight.
Shared values, norms, and beliefs influencing project execution.
Efficient and effective deployment and allocation of resources.

explanation: Capturing/sharing knowledge = knowledge management; standardizing PM = PMO; oversight roles/processes = governance framework; shared values/norms = organizational culture; deploying resources = resource management.

prompt: Match each person's description to the MBTI indicator.

items.text: John insists on following a detailed plan and strict deadlines, emphasizing efficiency and order.
Maria is known for her ability to connect with others.
Alex enjoys debating different approaches and thrives in brainstorming sessions.
David is methodical, relies on data and logical analysis, and prefers to work independently.

explanation: Detailed plans/deadlines/order = Judging; connecting with others = Feeling; debating/brainstorming with others = Extroversion; methodical, data-driven, prefers working alone = Introversion.

prompt: Match each item to the methodology that uses it.

items.text: Sprint Retrospective
PERT Analysis
Product Backlog / Scope Statement

explanation: Retrospectives = agile; PERT = traditional; a product backlog paired with a scope statement = hybrid.

prompt: Match each description to the MBTI indicator.

items.text: John enjoys thinking about the big picture and future possibilities; innovative and abstract.
Emily is empathetic, values team harmony, and creates a supportive environment.
Alex is adaptable, keeps options open, and is comfortable with last-minute changes.
Michael works independently, needs quiet reflection, and prefers one-on-one communication.

explanation: Big-picture/abstract = Intuition; empathy/harmony = Feeling; flexible/options-open = Perceiving; independent/quiet = Introversion.

prompt: Match each description to the MBTI indicator.

items.text: Makes decisions based on logical analysis and objective criteria.
Prefers brainstorming sessions and group discussions.
Focuses on abstract ideas and future possibilities.
Enjoys detailed project plans and sticking to schedules.
Enjoys working alone and thinking through problems internally.
Focuses on concrete information and practical solutions.
Likes to keep options open and adapt as the project progresses.
Makes decisions based on values and impact on people.

explanation: The four MBTI dichotomies: E/I energy, S/N information, T/F decisions, J/P lifestyle.

prompt: Match each risk category to its description.

items.text: Deals with the development and implementation of technology solutions.
Addresses the buying, selling, and economic aspects of the project.
Involves leadership and direction to meet project objectives.
Includes regulatory, environmental, and socio-political factors.

explanation: Technology = Technical; money/economics = Commercial; leadership/direction = Management; outside factors = External.

prompt: Match each agile term to its description.

items.text: List of criteria that a product should meet.
Capturing user requirements in non-technical language.
High-level description of the long-term goals and purpose of the product.

explanation: Completion criteria = Definition of Done; plain-language requirements = User Stories; long-term goals = Product Vision.

prompt: Match each task to the collaboration tool that best supports it.

items.text: Conduct virtual meetings and presentations with stakeholders in different locations.
Facilitate quick, real-time communication and brainstorming among team members.
Manage changes to documents and code, ensuring everyone uses the latest versions.
Post project updates and announcements for the team to access asynchronously.
Track project progress, assign tasks, and manage deadlines.

explanation: Virtual meetings = video conferencing; real-time chat = chat rooms; version consistency = version control; async announcements = bulletin boards; tasks/deadlines = PM software.

prompt: Match each agile meeting to its description.

items.text: The team demonstrates the work completed during the sprint to stakeholders.
Team members discuss progress, impediments, and plans.
The team defines the work to be accomplished in the upcoming sprint.
The team reflects on the past sprint to identify improvements.

explanation: Demo = Sprint Review; daily progress = Daily Standup; plan upcoming work = Sprint Planning; reflect = Retrospective.

prompt: Match each scenario to the technical practice/tool.

items.text: Visualizing the project schedule, showing task durations and dependencies.
Reviewing and approving changes to the project scope, schedule, or budget.
Defining roles and responsibilities for project tasks and deliverables.
Documenting what went well and what didn't at the end of a project phase.

explanation: Schedule = Gantt; approving changes = CCB; roles = RACI; what went well/wrong = Lessons Learned.

prompt: Match each description to the interpersonal skill.

items.text: Paying full attention and understanding the speaker's message.
Addressing and managing disagreements effectively.
Reaching mutually acceptable agreements among parties.
Persuading others to support a course of action.
Recognizing and managing one's own emotions and the emotions of others.

explanation: Full attention = active listening; managing disagreements = conflict resolution; mutual agreement = negotiation; persuading = influencing; managing emotions = emotional intelligence.

prompt: Match each responsibility to the Scrum role.

items.text: Removes impediments that hinder the team's progress.
Self-organizes to complete the sprint goal.
Prioritizes the backlog items.

explanation: Removing impediments = scrum master; self-organizing = development team; prioritizing backlog = product owner.

prompt: Match each item to the methodology it describes.

items.text: Early on, the PM and team identify risks, document them in the risk register, and develop mitigation strategies.
The PM uses integrated change control for the installation and a product backlog for development.
Midway through the sprint, the product owner and team review the upcoming user stories.

explanation: Upfront risk planning = traditional; change control + backlog = hybrid; sprint/user stories = agile.

prompt: Match each scenario to the agile meeting.

items.text: At the start of a new iteration, the team decides on tasks, reviews priorities, and assigns responsibilities.
During the iteration, the product owner and team refine the upcoming work and clarify user stories.
The team reflects on the past cycle to discuss successes, challenges, and improvements.

explanation: Deciding next iteration's work = sprint planning; refining upcoming backlog = backlog refinement; reflecting = retrospective.

prompt: Match each term to its description.

items.text: People start to fully apply themselves to a task just before the deadline.
Work expands to fill the time available for its completion.
Team members pad their estimates to protect themselves from potential delays.
A delay or failure occurs when responsibility is transferred between team members.

explanation: Last-minute effort = Student Syndrome; work fills time = Parkinson's Law; padding estimates = Self-Protection; handover failure = Dropped Baton.

prompt: Match each tool to its description.

items.text: Measuring project performance and progress against the plan.
Breaking down project deliverables into manageable tasks.
Adjusting task assignments so no one is overloaded.

explanation: Performance vs plan = EVM; decomposing deliverables = WBS; balancing assignments = resource leveling.

prompt: Match each term to its description.

items.text: Team members inflate time estimates to safeguard against unforeseen delays.
Delays or issues arising from inadequate handover of responsibilities between members.
Starting a task late due to waiting until the last minute, resulting in a rush.
The duration of work expands to match the time allotted for its completion.

explanation: Inflating estimates = Self-Protection; handover delays = Dropped Baton; last-minute start = Student Syndrome; work fills the time = Parkinson's Law.

prompt: Match each agile meeting to its description.

items.text: Showcases the work done during the sprint and gathers feedback.
Held after a sprint to discuss what went well, what didn't, and how to improve.
The team plans and commits to the work for the upcoming sprint.
Meeting for the team to update on progress and discuss any blockers.

explanation: Showcase/feedback = Sprint Review; improve process = Retrospective; commit to next sprint = Sprint Planning; daily progress = Daily Standup.

prompt: Match each description to the organizational structure.

items.text: Project managers have a little more power than the functional manager.
Team members have dual reporting lines.
Departments operate independently.
Project managers coordinate but functional managers retain control.
Employees are dedicated to projects and report to project managers.

explanation: PM > functional power = Strong Matrix; dual reporting = Matrix; independent departments = Functional; PM coordinates only = Weak Matrix; dedicated to PM = Projectized.

prompt: Match each document/tool to its description.

items.text: Defining and controlling project parameters.
Documenting insights for future reference.
Identifying and understanding stakeholders' interests and impacts.

explanation: Defining/controlling scope = scope baseline; capturing insights = lessons learned register; understanding stakeholders = stakeholder analysis.

prompt: Match each agile artifact to its description.

items.text: Understand the value.
What completed work means.
Long-term objectives.

explanation: Value = user stories; what done means = Definition of Done; long-term objectives = product vision.

prompt: Match each agile meeting to its action.

items.text: Present completed work to stakeholders for feedback.
Outline and agree on the tasks for the next sprint.
Share updates and identify any obstacles.
Reflect on the team's processes and outcomes.

explanation: Present work = Sprint Review; agree next tasks = Sprint Planning; updates/obstacles = Daily Standup; reflect = Retrospective.

prompt: Match each risk response to the action that describes it.

items.text: Take steps to reduce the likelihood or impact of the risk.
Acknowledge the risk without taking action, allowing the project to continue.
Shift the impact of the risk to a third party, typically through insurance or outsourcing.
Alter the project plan to eliminate the risk entirely.
Raise the risk to a higher authority when it exceeds the PM's control.

explanation: Reduce probability/impact = Mitigate; acknowledge, no action = Accept; shift to third party = Transfer; eliminate = Avoid; push to higher authority = Escalate.

prompt: Match each term to its description.

items.text: Regular updates and involvement for key stakeholders.
Formal process for submitting and approving change requests.
Hierarchy and reporting relationships outlined.
Regular risk assessments and mitigation plans in place.
Formal approvals required before starting tasks.

explanation: Engaging stakeholders = stakeholder engagement; approving changes = change control system; hierarchy/reporting = organizational structure; risk processes = risk management framework; authorizing work = work authorization system.

prompt: Match each item to the methodology that uses it.

items.text: Iterative / Incremental Development
Burn Down Chart
Scope Baseline

explanation: A burn down chart is agile and a scope baseline is traditional; the remaining item is sorted into hybrid.

prompt: Match each item to the methodology that uses it.

items.text: Risk Register
Adaptive Planning
Balanced Team Structure

explanation: Risk register = traditional; adaptive planning = agile; balanced team structure = hybrid.

prompt: Match each item to the methodology that uses it.

items.text: Tailored Approaches
CPM (Critical Path Method)
Product Backlog

explanation: Tailoring/blending = hybrid; CPM = traditional; product backlog = agile.

prompt: Match each risk example to its category.

items.text: Issues with software or hardware integration.
Currency exchange rate volatility.
Inadequate resource allocation.
Natural disasters or extreme weather events.

explanation: Integration = Technical; currency = Commercial; resourcing = Management; weather = External.

prompt: Match each responsibility to the Scrum role.

items.text: Designs and develops features according to the backlog.
Ensures that the team adheres to Scrum practices.
Creates and maintains the product backlog.

explanation: Building features = development team; enforcing Scrum = scrum master; owning backlog = product owner.

prompt: Match each term to its description.

items.text: Adding extra time to estimates to avoid potential overruns.
Issues arise due to poor handoff or transition of tasks between individuals.
The time required for a task will expand to the amount of time available.
Procrastinating until the deadline is near before beginning a task.

explanation: Buffering = Self-Protection; handoff problems = Dropped Baton; work fills time = Parkinson's Law; procrastination = Student Syndrome.

prompt: Match each risk category to its description.

items.text: Involves the application of specialized knowledge and skills to achieve project goals.
Focuses on market conditions, customer needs, and financial viability.
Encompasses the planning, organizing, and controlling of project resources.
Concerns factors outside the project that can influence its success.

explanation: Specialized knowledge = Technical; market/financial = Commercial; planning/organizing = Management; outside factors = External.

prompt: Match each impediment to its description.

items.text: Team members are unsure of project expectations.
Insufficient resources to complete tasks.
Uncontrolled changes or continuous growth in project scope.
Team members are not effectively sharing information.
An identified risk has materialized and is impacting the project.

explanation: Unclear expectations = lack of clear requirements; not enough resources = resource shortage; uncontrolled growth = scope creep; poor info flow = communication breakdown; materialized risk = risk realization.

prompt: Match each impediment to its description.

items.text: Team members are unsure of project expectations.
Insufficient resources to complete tasks.
Uncontrolled changes or continuous growth in project scope.
Team members are not effectively sharing information.
An identified risk has materialized and is impacting the project.

explanation: Unclear expectations = lack of clear requirements; not enough resources = resource shortage; uncontrolled growth = scope creep; poor info flow = communication breakdown; materialized risk = risk realization.

prompt: Match each risk response to the action that describes it.

items.text: Change the project plan to eliminate the risk.
Transfer the responsibility of the risk to a higher authority.
Acknowledge the risk but take no immediate action unless it occurs.
Shift the impact of the risk to a third party.
Take actions to reduce the likelihood or impact of the risk.

explanation: Eliminate = Avoid; push to higher authority = Escalate; acknowledge only = Accept; shift to third party = Transfer; reduce = Mitigate.

prompt: Match each description to the role (one option is a distractor).

items.text: Assigns team members and resources from their department to the project.
Oversees scope, schedule, cost, quality, and stakeholder management.
Represents the interests of the customer and end-users.
Gathers detailed requirements from stakeholders through interviews and workshops.
Ensures the team adheres to Agile principles and practices.

explanation: Departmental resources = functional manager; all constraints = project manager; customer voice = product owner; requirements = business analyst; Agile adherence = scrum master. (Project Sponsor is a distractor.)

prompt: Match each item to the methodology it describes.

items.text: Throughout the day, team members update the current status of their tasks for visibility.
At the end of each phase, the PM holds a review meeting and chooses a new methodology.
The PM uses the critical path method to identify the longest sequence of tasks.

explanation: Continuous status updates = agile; choosing approach per phase = hybrid; CPM = traditional.

prompt: Match each item to the methodology it describes.

items.text: The PM reviews the project schedule, tracks task dependencies, and ensures timely progress.
The PM uses blended reporting to report project progress.
After the sprint ends, the team holds a retrospective meeting to reflect on the past sprint.

explanation: Schedule/dependency tracking = traditional; blended reporting = hybrid; retrospective = agile.

prompt: Match each problem to the tool that helps with it.

items.text: No effective method for monitoring major task completion.
Unexpected events impact the project timeline.
Lack of timely information sharing leads to delays.
Essential team members are not available when needed.
Work creep (uncontrolled scope growth).

explanation: Major completions = milestone chart; unexpected events = risk register; info sharing = communication plan; resource availability = resource histogram; controlling scope = scope baseline.

prompt: Match each responsibility to the role.

items.text: Creates the project vision and goals for the team.
Plans and executes the tasks to meet the sprint goal.
Coaches the team in agile practices.

explanation: Vision/goals = product owner; sprint work = development team; coaching = scrum master.

prompt: Match each item to the methodology that uses it.

items.text: Kanban Board
CCB (Change Control Board)
Sprint Backlog / Scope Statement

explanation: Kanban = agile; CCB = traditional; sprint backlog + scope statement = hybrid.

prompt: Match each document to its description.

items.text: Documenting threats and planning mitigation strategies.
Managing modifications to scope, schedule, or budget.
Establishing protocols for information sharing.

explanation: Threats/mitigation = risk register; managing modifications = change control process; info-sharing = communication plan.

prompt: Match each action to the impediment it resolves.

items.text: Facilitate mediation to resolve issues and restore harmony.
Organize activities to boost team spirit and motivation.
Arrange training sessions or bring in specialists to address deficiencies.
Clearly define and communicate specific duties and roles.
Implement stricter timeline tracking and follow-up procedures.

explanation: Mediation = ongoing disputes; morale activities = low engagement; training = insufficient expertise; defining roles = unclear responsibilities; timeline tracking = missed deadlines.

prompt: Match each Tuckman stage to its description.

items.text: Team members are getting to know each other and understanding the project's goals.
Conflicts arise as team members express differing opinions and work styles.
The team starts to resolve conflicts and establish routines and norms.
The team operates efficiently towards goals with minimal supervision.
The project is completed and the team disbands, reflecting on the experience.

explanation: Getting acquainted = Forming; conflict = Storming; routines/norms = Norming; efficient = Performing; disbanding = Adjourning.

prompt: Match each EVM metric to its description.

items.text: Difference between planned and actual schedule.
Efficiency of budgeted cost expenditure.
Difference between budgeted and actual cost.
Efficiency of project schedule performance.

explanation: Variances are differences (SV schedule, CV cost); indices are efficiency ratios (SPI schedule, CPI cost).

prompt: Match each description to the role (some options are distractors).

items.text: Evaluates the performance of team members within their department.
Provides the necessary funding and resources for the project.
Empowers the team to self-organize and make decisions.

explanation: Departmental performance = functional manager; funding/resources = project sponsor; empowering self-organization = scrum master. (Product Owner and Business Analyst are distractors.)

prompt: Match each term to its description.

items.text: Processes for identifying, analyzing, and responding to project risks.
Actively managing stakeholder expectations and relationships.
Defines how activities are directed to achieve goals.
Defines how project work is assigned and authorized.

explanation: Risk processes = risk management framework; managing stakeholders = stakeholder engagement; directing activities = organizational structure; authorizing work = work authorization system.

prompt: Match each risk response to the action that describes it.

items.text: Implement actions to reduce the probability or impact of the risk.
Change the project plan to eliminate the risk or its impact.
Shift the risk to an insurance company.
Pass the risk to a higher authority.
Recognize the risk and decide to take no action unless it occurs.

explanation: Reduce = Mitigate; eliminate = Avoid; insurance = Transfer; higher authority = Escalate; no action unless it occurs = Accept.

prompt: Match each term to its description.

items.text: Estimating more time than necessary to create a buffer against delays.
Work duration increases to fill the available time.
Delaying the start of a task until the deadline is near, leading to rushed efforts.
Delays occur when tasks are not smoothly transitioned between team members.

explanation: Buffering = Self-Protection; work fills time = Parkinson's Law; last-minute start = Student Syndrome; handover delay = Dropped Baton.

prompt: Match each situation to the interpersonal skill it calls for.

items.text: Facilitate effective communication in a diverse, global project team.
Secure a better rate or terms with a key supplier for project materials.
Mediate a dispute between two team members over a project task approach.
Resolve a budget overrun using a management strategy.
Gain support for your project from influential internal stakeholders.

explanation: Global team = cultural awareness; supplier terms = negotiation; team dispute = conflict resolution; choosing a strategy = decision making; internal influence = political awareness.

prompt: Match each EVM metric to its description.

items.text: Ratio of earned value to actual cost, indicating cost efficiency.
Forecasted total project cost based on current performance trends.
Difference between the original budget and the estimated cost at completion.
Ratio of earned value to planned value, reflecting schedule efficiency.

explanation: EV/AC = CPI; forecast total cost = EAC; BAC minus EAC = VAC; EV/PV = SPI.

prompt: Match each situation to the interpersonal skill it calls for.

items.text: Reach an agreement with a client on project scope changes without impacting the budget.
Resolve a scheduling conflict between two departments competing for the same resource.
Select the best vendor from three proposals within a tight deadline.
Navigate office chatter to secure additional resources from a department head.
Adapt project communication styles to accommodate a team member's social preferences.

explanation: Agreeing terms = negotiation; competing departments = conflict resolution; choosing a vendor = decision making; office politics = political awareness; adapting style = cultural awareness.

prompt: Match each item to the tool it describes.

items.text: Visualizing work in progress.
Allocating fixed periods for activities.
Visual representation of task relationships.

explanation: Work-in-progress visualization = Kanban board; fixed time periods = time-boxing; task relationships/dependencies = PDM.

prompt: Match each situation to the formula/term.

items.text: Used to estimate task durations using optimistic, pessimistic, and most likely scenarios.
The value of work actually performed, expressed in terms of the budget.
The approved budget assigned to the work scheduled to be completed by a specific time.
A measure of cost efficiency, calculated as the ratio of earned value to actual cost.

explanation: Three-point estimate = PERT; work performed = EV; budget for scheduled work = PV; EV/AC = CPI.

prompt: Match each term to its description.

items.text: Team norms and behaviors guide project work.
Central oversight of all project practices.
Systems in place for sharing lessons learned.
Clearly defined decision-making processes.

explanation: Norms = organizational culture; central oversight = PMO; sharing lessons = knowledge management; decision processes = governance framework.

prompt: Match each EVM rule of thumb to the metric it describes.

items.text: A negative value is bad (behind schedule).
A positive value is good (under budget).
1 or greater is ahead of schedule.
Under 1 is over budget.

explanation: Negative SV = behind schedule; positive CV = under budget; SPI >= 1 = ahead of schedule; CPI < 1 = over budget.

prompt: Interpret each EVM value.

items.text: SV is -200
SPI is 1.4
CPI is 1.5
CV is -70

explanation: Negative SV = behind/over schedule; SPI>1 = ahead of schedule; CPI>1 = under budget; negative CV = over budget.

prompt: Match each description to the organizational structure.

items.text: The project manager has full authority over the project and resources.
Employees are grouped by specialization and report to a functional manager.
Project managers have limited authority.
Project managers have considerable authority.

explanation: Full authority = projectized; grouped under functional managers = functional; limited authority = weak matrix; considerable authority = strong matrix.

prompt: Match each task to the collaboration tool that best supports it.

items.text: Share and archive project documentation and reference materials.
Plan and allocate resources, monitor task dependencies, and track project milestones.
Host remote training sessions or workshops for team members and stakeholders.

explanation: Archiving docs = bulletin boards; resources/dependencies/milestones = PM software; remote training = video conferencing.

prompt: Match each term to its description.

items.text: A disruption or delay caused by poor handoff or transition of tasks between team members.
Individuals add extra time to their task estimates to avoid the risk of missing deadlines.
Procrastination where work is delayed until the last possible moment before the deadline.
Tasks take longer to complete if more time is allocated to them.

explanation: Handover delay = Dropped Baton; padding = Self-Protection; last-minute = Student Syndrome; work fills time = Parkinson's Law.

prompt: Match each description to the schedule tool.

items.text: Visualizing the project schedule and tracking task dependencies.
Identifying the longest sequence of dependent tasks and optimizing the schedule.
Estimating the amount of time to complete an activity.

explanation: Schedule/dependencies = Gantt; longest dependent sequence = CPM; activity duration = PERT.

prompt: Match each description to the schedule tool/technique.

items.text: Tracking key events and progress.
Finding the longest amount of time to complete a project.
Overlapping activities to shorten duration.

explanation: Key events = milestone chart; longest path = CPM; overlapping to compress = fast tracking.

prompt: Match each description to the agile/XP technical practice.

items.text: Writing assessments (tests) before developing code to ensure functionality.
A time-boxed research task to explore or investigate solutions.
Ongoing integration, testing, and delivery of software during development.

explanation: Tests before code = TDD; time-boxed investigation = Spike; ongoing integrate/test/deliver = Continuous Delivery.
